rules.description: CLI command-form rule (follows pi's own convention; grounded in R-0006). Decide bare-subcommand vs flag by the nature of the capability, first match wins: (1) a distinct self-completing action that runs and exits — interactive or not (pi's `config` is a bare verb, never `--config`) -> BARE SUBCOMMAND; (2) the object/target a verb acts on -> POSITIONAL (e.g. `update <source|self|pi>`); (3) a modifier of HOW a verb runs (a toggle or target-mode of that verb) -> FLAG ON THAT VERB (e.g. `update --dry-run`); (4) a modifier, mode-selector, or one-shot short-circuit of the DEFAULT run -> TOP-LEVEL FLAG (e.g. `--model`, `--export`). Litmus: is it its own run-and-exit action, or an option/mode of an existing action? This governs every pi-context command form: reflected ops and `pi-context update` are bare verbs; `update --dry-run` is a verb flag; an alternate run-and-exit mode such as launching a bounded pi session is a bare verb (`pi-context pi-bound`), not a `--flag`. Apply it when adding any pi-context command; do not decide command form ad hoc.

Feature implementation happens on a branch off the active integration branch and merges back to it. The integration branch is whatever the current arc is based on — context-jit-spec-v2 now, main or a dev branch later; the rule is general, not tied to a specific branch name. Split: CODE goes on the feature branch (packages/**; dist is gitignored, so rebuild on switch). The .context SUBSTRATE stays single-writer on the integration branch — file the gap/task/criteria there BEFORE branching, and run the closure cascade (verification + complete-task + close-gap) there AFTER the code merges and re-verifies, never on the feature branch — so JSON-array block merges never conflict and a rejected feature simply leaves its task planned. Accept: checkout the integration branch, merge the feature branch, npm run build, then file the closure. Reject: checkout the integration branch and delete the feature branch — no reverts, no dirty state. Establish a clean tree before branching; implement via a foreground coding subagent. Pure substrate/planning work (filing gaps/decisions/research) is done directly on the integration branch and needs no feature branch.

Decompose a feature into tasks by these rules. Do not decompose ad hoc.
1. The tasks come from the gaps the feature addresses. Start from that gap set.
2. State the invariant the feature must never violate (e.g. "never overwrite a user's customizations"). Every task must preserve it.
3. For each gap, name the single code location its fix edits.
4. Gaps whose fixes edit the same code location become one task.
5. Gaps whose fixes edit different code locations become separate tasks.
6. A task is one change that one coding-agent brief implements and one runtime demo plus one adversarial audit proves. If a candidate task needs two briefs, split it. If two candidate tasks edit the same location, merge them.
7. If a fix needs a design choice that is not yet made, file the choice as a decision first. The task that depends on it stays unwritten until that decision is filed.
8. If a fix cannot preserve the invariant in rule 2, it is its own task.
9. Order the tasks: a task others build on comes first — the command before the behaviors it carries. Record order with task_depends_on_task edges.
10. Wire every task: task_addresses_feature, task_addresses_gap for each gap it closes, item_governed_by_convention, and task_governed_by_decision when a decision gates it.
